review_summary: The transcript clearly grounds several ambiguity examples and one information-extraction application, so five reinforcement questions can be generated without a separate clip.

question_bank.question: In the sentence “Scientists count whales from space,” which interpretation results if “from space” attaches to the verb phrase?
choices: The counting happens from space
The whales are from space
Scientists are part of the whales
The sentence becomes ungrammatical
question_bank.explanation: When the prepositional phrase attaches to the verb event, it describes how or where the counting occurs rather than describing the whales.

question_bank.question: What does it mean if “from space” attaches to the noun in “Scientists count whales from space”?
choices: It describes the whales as being from space
It says the scientists are counting by using a space telescope
It means count is the head of space
It removes the ambiguity completely
question_bank.explanation: If the phrase attaches to the noun, it describes the noun phrase itself, giving the reading of “space whales.”

question_bank.question: Why do multiple successive prepositional phrases make parsing hard?
choices: Because each PP may attach to different earlier material, creating many possible readings
Because English grammar forbids more than one PP in a sentence
Because PPs always cross one another in dependency trees
Because PPs cannot modify nouns
question_bank.explanation: Each additional PP can often attach in more than one place, so the number of valid interpretations grows quickly.

question_bank.question: What is coordination scope ambiguity?
choices: An ambiguity about whether a coordinated phrase describes one entity or multiple entities
An ambiguity about whether a word is singular or plural
An ambiguity caused only by punctuation errors
An ambiguity about whether a sentence has a verb
question_bank.explanation: Coordination scope ambiguity changes whether the coordinated material is grouped together as one larger description or interpreted as multiple separate descriptions.

question_bank.question: How can dependency patterns help information extraction?
choices: By locating key verbs and their arguments, including coordinated arguments
By replacing all parsing with random word matching
By ignoring syntax and using only document length
By forcing every extracted fact to come from a noun phrase only
question_bank.explanation: Dependency structure can identify who did what to whom, so it is useful for extracting structured relations such as scientific interactions.
